sections.heading: Taking investment instrument or intermediated security free
sections.title: Taking investment instruments or intermediated securities free
sections.summary: This section specifies conditions under which investment instruments or intermediated securities can be taken free of security interests. It outlines the circumstances where such assets are no longer subject to a security interest, typically when the security interest is no longer enforceable or has been discharged.

requirements.text: Investment instruments or intermediated securities can be taken free of security interests under specified conditions

sections.source_quote: Taking investment instrument or intermediated security free
sections.relevance_reason: Purely financial instruments, no data privacy link

sections.heading: Execution creditor has priority over unperfected security
sections.title: Execution creditor priority over unperfected security
sections.summary: This section establishes that an execution creditor has priority over unperfected security interests when enforcing a security interest. It ensures that creditors who have executed on collateral can take precedence over less perfected security interests.

requirements.text: Execution creditor has priority over unperfected security interests when enforcing a security interest

sections.source_quote: Execution creditor has priority over unperfected security
sections.relevance_reason: Unrelated to data compliance

sections.heading: Rights on transfer of account or chattel paper—contractual
sections.title: Contractual rights on transfer of account or chattel paper
sections.summary: This section details contractual rights that arise when accounts or chattel paper are transferred. It specifies the conditions under which parties can retain rights to the transferred property without a security interest.

requirements.text: Contractual rights on transfer of account or chattel paper are established under specific conditions

sections.source_quote: Rights on transfer of account or chattel paper—contractual
sections.relevance_reason: pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: Priority of purchase money security interest in processed or
sections.title: Priority of purchase money security interest in processed property
sections.summary: This section addresses the priority of purchase money security interests in processed or commingled personal property. It states that such security interests take priority over other unperfected interests in the property.

requirements.text: Purchase money security interest in processed or commingled property takes priority over unperfected interests

sections.source_quote: Priority of purchase money security interest in processed or
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Section 1
sections.title: Personal Property Securities Act 2009: General application rules
sections.summary: This section outlines the general application of the Personal Property Securities Act 2009, including which security interests are covered by the Act. It specifies that the Act applies to security interests in goods, financial property, and intangible property under certain conditions, such as the location of the property or the entity involved.

requirements.text: This Act applies to security interests in goods or financial property if located in Australia or granted by an Australian entity
This Act applies to security interests in intangible property if the grantor is an Australian entity or the property is payable in Australia
This Act applies to security interests in intermediated securities if the intermediary is located in Australia or the grantor is an Australian entity

sections.source_quote: This Act applies to a security interest in goods or financial property if: (a) the location of the goods or property is in Australia; or (b) the grantor is an Australian entity.
sections.relevance_reason: Applies to financial data under Australian jurisdiction

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of financial and property terms in the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act
sections.summary: This section defines key terms related to financial and property matters, including accounts, ADI accounts, advances, and security interests. It specifies that a security interest secures payment or performance of an obligation and includes certain transactions like transfers of accounts and consignments. The definitions cover terms such as Australian entities, bankruptcy, business days, and collateral, with examples provided for clarity.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish the legal framework for understanding financial and property terms critical to data transfers involving security interests, accounts, and collateral. They help clarify what constitutes personal property and security interests that may be relevant to cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: A security interest is an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that secures payment or the performance of an obligation.

Certain transactions that do not secure payment or the performance of an obligation may also give rise to a security interest: transfers of accounts, consignments and certain long-term leases and bailments (called PPS leases).
An account means a monetary obligation arising from disposing of property, granting a right, or providing services in the ordinary course of business.
An ADI account means an account kept by a person with an ADI that is payable on demand or at some time in the future.

Advance includes any liability of a debtor to pay interest, credit costs and other charges or costs payable by the debtor in connection with the advance or the enforcement of a security interest securing the advance.
After-acquired property means personal property acquired by the grantor after a security agreement is made.
Collateral means personal property to which a security interest is attached.
Commercial consignment means a consignment if the consignor retains an interest in goods delivered to the consignee for the purpose of sale, lease or other disposal.
Commercial property means personal property other than consumer property.
Crops means crops that have not been harvested, including products of agriculture or aquaculture.
Currency means currency authorised as a medium of exchange by the law of Australia or of any other country.
Debtor means a person who owes payment or performance of an obligation secured by a security interest in personal property.
Defect, in relation to a registration, includes an irregularity, omission or error in the registration.
Description of personal property (including collateral and proceeds) means a description that identifies the item or class of personal property.
Document of title means a writing issued by or addressed to a bailee that covers goods in the bailee's possession.
Financial product means any of the following, or an interest in any of the following, other than cash: shares, bonds, any other financial instrument, any other financial asset.
Financial property means any of the following personal property: chattel paper, currency, document of title, investment instrument, negotiable instrument.
Financing statement means data registered (or that is to be registered) pursuant to an application for registration under subsection 150(1).

Future advance means an advance secured by a security interest made after the security agreement was made, or expenses in relation to the enforcement of a security interest that are secured by the security interest.
Goods means personal property that is tangible property, including crops, livestock, wool, minerals, satellites and other space objects.
Grantor means a person who has the interest in the personal property to which a security interest is attached.
Intangible property means personal property that is not financial property, goods, or an intermediated security.

Intellectual property means any of the following rights: design rights, patent rights, trade mark rights, plant breeder's rights, circuit layout rights, copyright rights, and rights under foreign laws.
Intellectual property licence means an authority or licence to exercise rights comprising intellectual property.

Investment instrument means any of the following financial products: shares, debentures, bonds, derivatives, foreign exchange contracts, assignable options, managed investment schemes, units in shares, financial products traded on Australian markets, and combinations of financial products.

sections.source_quote: Section 10: A security interest is an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that secures payment or the performance of an obligation. The form of the transaction and the identity of the person who has title to the property do not affect whether an interest is a security interest. Certain transactions that do not secure payment or the performance of an obligation may also give rise to a security interest: transfers of accounts, consignments and certain long-term leases and bailments (called PPS leases).
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential financial definitions

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of security interests and personal property terms
sections.summary: This section defines key terms related to security interests and personal property under Australian law. It specifies that a security interest secures payment or performance of an obligation without regard to transaction form. It lists examples including fixed charges, floating charges, chattel mortgages, and conditional sales. The section also clarifies that security interests exclude licenses and certain regulated interests, and specifies conditions under which security interests are created.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines critical terms for data transfer impact assessments, particularly regarding security interests and personal property. It establishes the scope of what constitutes a security interest and excludes certain types of interests, which is relevant for determining data transfer mechanisms and safeguards.

requirements.text: A security interest means an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that secures payment or performance of an obligation

A security interest includes fixed charges, floating charges, chattel mortgages, conditional sales, hire purchases, pledges, trust receipts, consignments, leases of goods, assignments, transfers of title, and flawed asset arrangements
A security interest also includes interests of transferees under transfers of accounts or chattel paper
A security interest includes interests of consignors under commercial consignments
A security interest includes interests of lessors or bailors under PPS leases
A person who owes payment to another may take a security interest in the other's right to payment
An account debtor may take a security interest in an account or chattel paper
An ADI may take a security interest in an ADI account
A security interest does not include licenses
A security interest does not include interests prescribed by regulations
A security interest is not created by agreements to postpone or subordinate rights
A security interest is not created by agreements to subordinate secured parties' rights

sections.source_quote: Section 12: A security interest means an interest in personal property provided for by a transaction that, in substance, secures payment or performance of an obligation (without regard to the form of the transaction or the identity of the person who has title to the property).
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: security interests not directly related to data protection

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of intermediary and securities account
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes an intermediary and a securities account. An intermediary is only active in that role when acting as such, and a person is not considered an intermediary merely because they maintain a securities account for an issuer. A securities account is defined as an account where interests in financial products can be credited or debited, or for intermediaries, a record of holdings and transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key terms for security interests, critical for understanding who qualifies as an intermediary and how securities accounts function in cross-border data contexts.

requirements.text: An intermediary is only active in that role while acting as an intermediary
A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because they maintain a securities account on behalf of an issuer
A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because they act as a central securities depository, registrar or transfer agent for an issuer
A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because they record details of interests in financial products in their own books in an administrative capacity
A securities account means an account to which interests in financial products may be credited or debited
For intermediaries, a securities account means a record of holdings and transfers of interests in financial products

sections.source_quote: Section 15(4) to (7) of the Act: An intermediary (including a central securities depository) is an intermediary only while acting in the capacity of an intermediary. A person is not an intermediary for the purposes of paragraph (2)(a) or (c) merely because the person maintains a securities account on behalf of the issuer of the financial products to which the account relates. A securities account means: (a) an account to which interests in financial products may be credited or debited; or (b) in the case of an intermediary mentioned in paragraph 15(2)(b)—a record of holdings and transfers of interests in financial products.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines intermediary and securities account for financial data

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interest perfection rules for collateral
sections.summary: This section outlines how security interests are perfected for different types of collateral. Perfection occurs through registration, possession, or control of collateral. It specifies that a security interest in goods possessed by a bailee can be perfected through registration, possession, or by issuing documents of title. The section also details temporary perfection for negotiable documents of title in transit.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for perfecting security interests across different collateral types, critical for assessing data transfer risks when security interests are involved in cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if it is temporarily perfected or otherwise perfected by force of the Act
A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if the security interest is attached to the collateral and is enforceable against a third party
A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if registration is effective with respect to the collateral
A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if the secured party has possession of the collateral (other than possession as a result of seizure or repossession)

A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if the secured party has control of the collateral for ADI accounts, intermediated securities, investment instruments, or negotiable instruments not evidenced by a certificate

A security interest that has attached to goods in the possession of a bailee is perfected if the security interest is perfected by registration, possession, or by issuing a document of title to the goods in the name of the secured party

A security interest in goods in the possession of a bailee is temporarily perfected for the period starting when the bailee issues a negotiable document of title and ending when the secured party takes possession of the document
The security interest in goods becomes unperfected at the end of the 5 business day period unless perfected otherwise

sections.source_quote: Section 21(1) to (4) of the Act: A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if: (a) the security interest is temporarily perfected, or otherwise perfected, by force of this Act; or (b) all of the following apply: (i) the security interest is attached to the collateral; (ii) the security interest is enforceable against a third party; (iii) subsection (2) applies. A security interest in particular collateral is perfected if the secured party has possession of the collateral (other than possession as a result of seizure or repossession).
sections.relevance_reason: Pure security interest perfection rules, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Control of investment instruments and negotiable instruments
sections.summary: This section defines when a person has control over investment instruments, including those evidenced by certificates or not. Control is established through registration as the registered owner, possession of the instrument, or an agreement allowing the person to initiate transfers or deal with the instrument. The rules specify conditions for control in different scenarios, including electronic messaging and agreements with issuers.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines control mechanisms for investment instruments, critical for determining data transfer authority and cross-border data handling in financial instruments.

requirements.text: A person has control of an investment instrument if the issuer registers the person as the registered owner of the instrument
A person has control of an investment instrument evidenced by a certificate if they possess it and can transfer or deal with it
A person has control of an investment instrument not evidenced by a certificate if there is an agreement allowing them to initiate or control sending instructions for transfers
A secured party has control of a negotiable instrument not evidenced by a certificate if it can be transferred via clearing facility rules and there is an agreement controlling electronic messages

sections.source_quote: Section 27(2): A person, other than the debtor or grantor, has control of an investment instrument if the issuer of the instrument registers the person as the registered owner of the instrument.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines control conditions for financial instruments

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Temporary perfection of security interests in collateral
sections.summary: This section outlines temporary perfection rules for security interests in collateral when goods or instruments are returned to the grantor or debtor. It specifies 5-business-day temporary perfection periods for security interests in goods, negotiable instruments, and investment instruments following actions like sale, exchange, or repossession. The security interest reattaches to the collateral if the goods are returned to the grantor or debtor under specific circumstances.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines temporary perfection periods for security interests in returned collateral, critical for assessing data transfer risks when assets are repossessed or transferred across borders.

requirements.text: A security interest in goods temporarily perfected for 5 business days after possession is given to the grantor or debtor for sale, exchange, or other preparation actions

A security interest in negotiable instruments or investment instruments temporarily perfected for 5 business days after possession or control is given to the grantor or debtor for sale, exchange, presentation, collection, renewal, or registration
The security interest reattaches to goods if the grantor or debtor repossesses them after a sale or lease

sections.source_quote: Section 35(1): A security interest in goods that is perfected by possession... is temporarily perfected for the period covered by subsection (2) if possession... is given to the grantor or the debtor at a particular time for the purpose of sale, exchange, or other actions.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial security interest mechanics, no data handling

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Deemed goods security interest and temporary perfection periods
sections.summary: This section establishes that when goods are transferred in a sale or lease and later returned to the transferor or transferee, the transferee acquires a deemed security interest in the goods under specific circumstances. The security interest attaches at repossession time and is temporarily perfected for 5 business days from that time. If not perfected within this period, it becomes unperfected. The section also outlines conditions for continuous perfection when collateral is relocated to Australia, including requirements for effectiveness and enforceability against third parties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines temporary perfection periods for security interests in goods and collateral relocated to Australia, impacting how data transfers across borders must be structured to maintain security interest validity without prolonged perfection.

requirements.text: If a sale or lease creates an account or chattel paper and it is transferred, the transferee has a deemed security interest in the goods if they return to transferor or transferee at repossession time
The deemed security interest attaches to goods at repossession time
The deemed security interest is temporarily perfected for 5 business days from repossession time if perfected by possession or registration
The deemed security interest becomes unperfected after 5 business days unless otherwise perfected
A security interest in collateral relocated to Australia is continuously perfected if effective and enforceable against third parties before relocation
Continuous perfection period ends when collateral becomes located in Australia
Security interest is temporarily perfected for 56 days or 5 business days after relocation to Australia, whichever is earlier

sections.source_quote: If a sale or lease of goods creates an account or chattel paper, and the account or chattel paper is transferred to another person, the transferee is taken to have a security interest in the goods if, at a particular time (the repossession time) the goods come into the possession of the transferor, or of the transferee, in any of the following circumstances: (a) in the case of a sale—the contract of sale is rescinded; (b) in the case of a lease—the lease expires or is rescinded; (c) the transferee seizes the goods in the exercise of a right in enforcing a security agreement; (d) the transferor repossesses the goods in the exercise of a right in enforcing the contract of sale or the lease; (e) any other circumstances prescribed by the regulations.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Taking personal property free of security interests
sections.summary: This section outlines rules for when personal property can be acquired free of security interests, including unperfected interests, serial number defects, motor vehicles, and transactions in the ordinary course of business. It specifies that buyers or lessees take property free of unperfected security interests unless they are parties to the transaction creating the interest. The rules also address exceptions for serial number errors, motor vehicles, and personal property used for domestic purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs when data transfers can occur without security interest interference, critical for cross-border data transfers involving personal property where security interests might otherwise impede data flow.

requirements.text: A buyer or lessee takes personal property free of unperfected security interest if not party to transaction creating it
Buyer or lessee takes property free of security interest if serial number correct and not registered
Motor vehicle buyer takes it free of security interest if serial number correct and seller is authorized
Buyer takes personal property free of security interest in ordinary course of business
Buyer takes personal property free of security interest for domestic use if value ≤ $5,000
Holder takes currency free of security interest if no knowledge of security interest

sections.source_quote: A buyer or lessee of personal property, for value, takes the personal property free of an unperfected security interest in the property.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interest priority in financial transactions
sections.summary: This section establishes rules for determining priority between competing security interests in financial collateral. It specifies that perfected security interests take priority over unperfected ones, and that perfected interests are prioritized based on the order of perfection. The rules also address how security interests in proceeds of collateral are treated relative to the original collateral, and include exceptions for purchase money security interests and control-based perfection.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by defining priority rules for security interests across borders, particularly for financial collateral and proceeds. It requires careful assessment of security interest perfection methods and timing to avoid conflicts in cross-border transactions.

requirements.text: A perfected security interest in collateral has priority over an unperfected security interest in the same collateral
Priority between unperfected security interests in the same collateral is determined by the order of attachment
A security interest perfected by control has priority over a security interest perfected by other means
A security interest in proceeds of original collateral has the same priority as the security interest in the original collateral
A purchase money security interest in inventory has priority over other perfected security interests in the same inventory
A purchase money security interest in personal property has priority over other perfected security interests if perfected by registration within 15 business days
A non-purchase money security interest in an account as original collateral has priority over a purchase money security interest in the account as proceeds of inventory
A notice must be given to the secured party holding a purchase money security interest at least 15 business days before registration

sections.source_quote: Section 55(3): A perfected security interest in collateral has priority over an unperfected security interest in the same collateral.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial governance, not direct data privacy/security

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Priority of security interests in transferred collateral
sections.summary: This section establishes priority rules between security interests (transferor-granted and transferee-granted) when collateral is transferred. It specifies conditions under which the transferor-granted interest has priority, including continuous perfection since transfer, and when the transferee-granted interest takes priority after re-perfection and notice to other secured parties. The rules also address exceptions for specific scenarios like negotiable instruments and chattel paper.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines priority rules for security interests in transferred collateral, which impacts cross-border data transfers involving financial assets by determining which security interest takes precedence in jurisdictions with similar registration systems.

requirements.text: The transferor-granted interest has priority if it was perfected immediately before transfer and has been continuously perfected since transfer.

The transferor-granted interest has priority if it was perfected by registration immediately before transfer, becomes unperfected, is later re-perfected, and notice is given to all other secured parties who have registration describing the collateral.

The transferee-granted interest has priority if the transferor-granted interest is re-perfected, the transferee acquires collateral without actual or constructive knowledge of breach, and the transferee-granted interest secures performance of an advance or obligation.
The interest of a creditor who receives payment of a debt through electronic funds transfer has priority over security interests in funds paid and intangible sources.
A person who acquires a negotiable instrument or interest in one has priority over perfected security interests if they gave value and acquired without actual or constructive knowledge of breach.
A person who acquires chattel paper in the ordinary course of business for new value has priority over perfected security interests in the chattel paper.
The holder of a negotiable document of title has priority over perfected security interests if they gave value and acquired without actual or constructive knowledge of breach.
An execution creditor has priority over unperfected security interests in collateral at the time of seizure or execution.
A perfected security interest held by an ADI in an ADI account has priority over other perfected security interests in the ADI account.

sections.source_quote: Section 66: The transferor-granted interest has priority if it was perfected immediately before the transfer and has been continuously perfected since the transfer.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial collateral security rules, no data privacy/cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Transfer of interests in collateral: rights and conditions for transferees
sections.summary: This section outlines the rights of transferees of accounts or chattel paper, subject to contractual terms and defences between the account debtor and transferor. It specifies that modifications to the underlying contract are effective against transferees if made honestly, commercially reasonably, and without material adverse effects. Payments by the account debtor to the transferor continue until notified otherwise, and payments to transferees discharge obligations to that extent.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for transferring collateral interests across borders, including contractual defences, payment continuity, and modification rules that impact cross-border security interest transfers.

requirements.text: Rights of transferee are subject to contractual terms and defences between account debtor and transferor
Modification of contract is effective against transferee if made honestly, commercially reasonably, and without material adverse effects
Payments by account debtor to transferor continue until notified otherwise
Payment to transferee discharges obligation to that extent

sections.source_quote: Rights of transferee subject to: (a) terms of contract between account debtor and transferor; (b) any other equity, defence or claim of account debtor against transferor.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial collateral transfer mechanics, no data protection angle

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interests in agricultural and processed goods
sections.summary: This section details rules for security interests in agricultural products, accessions, and processed goods. It specifies that security interests in crops while growing or livestock products before shorn may attach, and establishes priority rules for security interests in crops and livestock. It also outlines conditions for removing accessions without causing damage to other goods.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs cross-border security interests in agricultural and processed goods, including attachment conditions and priority rules that affect data transfer across borders involving physical assets.

requirements.text: Security interest may attach to crops while growing
Security interest may attach to livestock products before shorn
Security interest in crops has priority if granted for value to enable production
Security interest in livestock has priority if granted for value to enable feeding/development
Secured party must remove accession without causing damage to other goods

sections.source_quote: A security interest may attach to crops while they are growing.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Priority rules for security interests in processed or commingled goods
sections.summary: This section establishes priority rules for security interests in processed or commingled goods. It limits the priority of any security interest to the value of the goods on the day they became part of the product or mass. It also specifies that perfected security interests have priority over unperfected ones, and outlines how multiple perfected or unperfected interests share the product or mass proportionally based on the obligations they secure. The obligation secured by a security interest does not exceed the value of the goods on the day they became part of the product or mass.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by establishing priority rules for security interests in processed or commingled goods, which could impact how data is shared or transferred across borders when goods are processed or commingled.

requirements.text: Any priority that a security interest continuing in the product or mass has over another security interest in the product or mass is limited to the value of the goods on the day they became part of the product or mass.
A perfected security interest continuing in a product or mass has priority over an unperfected security interest continuing in the same product or mass.

If more than one perfected security interest continues in the same product or mass, each perfected security interest is entitled to share in the product or mass according to the ratio that the obligation secured by the perfected security interest bears to the sum of the obligations secured by all perfected security interests in the same product or mass.

If more than one unperfected security interest continues in the same product or mass, each unperfected security interest is entitled to share in the product or mass according to the ratio that the obligation secured by the unperfected security interest bears to the sum of the obligations secured by all unperfected security interests in the same product or mass.
The obligation secured by a security interest does not exceed the value of the goods on the day the goods became part of the product or mass.

sections.source_quote: Any priority that a security interest continuing in the product or mass has over another security interest in the product or mass is limited to the value of the goods on the day on which they became part of the product or mass.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Enforcement of security interests in personal property
sections.summary: This section outlines the rights and remedies for enforcing security interests in personal property. It specifies that a secured party may deal with collateral only to the extent the grantor would be entitled to do so, with exceptions for when the secured party had title to the collateral or when a transfer by the grantor would be prohibited. It also states that rights and remedies under this Chapter are cumulative and that recovering judgment or issuing execution does not extinguish a security interest in collateral.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it governs the enforcement of security interests in personal property, which could impact how data is handled or transferred across borders when security interests are involved.

requirements.text: In exercising rights and remedies provided by this Chapter, a secured party may deal with collateral only to the same extent as the grantor would be entitled to so deal with the collateral.
Subsection (1) does not apply if the secured party had title to the collateral immediately before starting to exercise any right or remedy provided by this Part.

Subsection (1) does not apply to the extent that it would otherwise prevent the secured party from dealing with the collateral by way of transfer because a transfer by the grantor would be prohibited or declared to be a default under a security agreement.
A secured party may only seize, purchase or dispose of a licence subject to the terms and conditions of the licence and any applicable law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory.
The fact that a secured party has recovered judgment, or issued execution, against a grantor in relation to collateral does not extinguish the security interest in the collateral.
The rights and remedies provided by this Chapter are cumulative.

sections.source_quote: In exercising rights and remedies provided by this Chapter, a secured party may deal with collateral only to the same extent as the grantor would be entitled to so deal with the collateral.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure enforcement of security interests, no data handling

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Enforcement of security interests in personal property
sections.summary: This section outlines how a secured party may enforce a security interest in personal property, mirroring land law enforcement mechanisms with necessary modifications. It specifies that enforcement proceeds must follow the same legal principles as land law, with the exception of this section. The secured party must notify higher priority parties and the grantor before taking enforcement actions, and the court may intervene to modify enforcement procedures. The section also clarifies that the law does not affect existing land laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes that security interests in personal property can be enforced similarly to land interests, requiring notification to higher priority parties and the grantor before enforcement actions. It impacts DTIAs by mandating clear communication channels and potential judicial oversight during enforcement.

requirements.text: A secured party may enforce the security interest in the same way as the interest in land may be enforced under land law with necessary modifications
Law in the same terms as land law applies for enforcement of security interests in personal property
The secured party must notify higher priority parties with a security interest in the collateral that has higher priority
The secured party must notify the grantor of any action taken in accordance with enforcement procedures
The secured party must give written notice to higher priority parties at least 10 business days before taking enforcement action
The secured party must apply any proceeds from collateral disposal towards the secured obligation

sections.source_quote: The secured party may enforce the security interest in the same way, with any necessary modification, as the interest in the land may be enforced under the land law.
sections.relevance_reason: Security interests not data protection

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Secured party's duty to obtain market value before disposing collateral
sections.summary: A secured party disposing of collateral under section 128 (excluding purchase) must exercise reasonable care to obtain at least the market value of the collateral before disposal. This duty applies to the secured party and the grantor, and requires the secured party to consider the collateral's market value at the time of disposal to ensure they obtain the best possible price. The duty does not apply to disposals by purchase as specified in the note.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a duty for secured parties to maximize value before disposing collateral, which impacts data transfer impact assessments when collateral includes personal data. The obligation to obtain market value affects how data is valued and transferred across borders.

requirements.text: A secured party who disposes of collateral under section 128 (other than by purchasing the collateral) owes a duty to any other person with a security interest in the collateral, and to the grantor, immediately before the disposal, to exercise all reasonable care
If the collateral has a market value at the time of disposal, the secured party must obtain at least that market value
Otherwise, the secured party must obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time of disposal, considering existing circumstances

sections.source_quote: A secured party who disposes of collateral under section 128 (other than by purchasing the collateral) owes a duty, to any other person with a security interest in the collateral, and to the grantor, immediately before the disposal, to exercise all reasonable care: (a) if the collateral has a market value at the time of disposal—to obtain at least that market value; or (b) otherwise—to obtain the best price that is reasonably obtainable at the time of disposal, having regard to the circumstances existing at that time.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial collateral disposal rule, no data handling

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Secured party's statement of account obligations after collateral disposal
sections.summary: A secured party must provide a written statement of account to any other person with a security interest in the collateral or the grantor within 20 business days after request. The statement must detail the total amount received from disposal, expenses incurred, amounts paid to other secured parties, and the balance owed to the grantor or debtor. This applies to disposals under section 128, including purchases, and must be provided before the end of the 20-day period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed accounting for collateral disposals, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments involving financial data. The requirement to provide transparent statements affects how financial data is shared and tracked across borders.

requirements.text: A secured party must, on request by any other person with a security interest in the collateral, or the grantor, give a written statement of account if the secured party disposes of collateral under section 128 (including by purchasing the collateral in accordance with section 129)
A statement of account must be given to a person before the end of 20 business days after the day the person requests the statement
The statement must show the total amount received from disposal during the period starting when the secured party seized the collateral and ending at the time of disposal
The statement must show the amount of expenses relating to the disposal
The statement must show any amounts paid to other secured parties
The statement must show the balance owing by the secured party to the grantor, or by the debtor to the secured party

sections.source_quote: A secured party must, on request by any other person with a security interest in the collateral, or the grantor, give the person (or grantor) a written statement of account, if the first-mentioned secured party disposes of collateral under section 128 (including by purchasing the collateral in accordance with section 129).
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential financial data procedure

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security agreement redemption and reinstatement rules
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions for redeeming collateral under a security agreement after default, including payment of arrears and enforcement expenses. It specifies that redemption is prohibited if written agreement to not redeem exists post-default. The grantor retains priority over other parties' redemption rights. The section also details procedures for reinstating security agreements before collateral disposal, requiring payment of arrears, enforcement expenses, and remediation of defaults.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by establishing conditions for collateral redemption and security agreement reinstatement, which may impact cross-border transfers involving secured assets or collateral.

requirements.text: Pay amounts in arrears (disregarding acceleration clause results) to discharge obligations
Pay enforcement expenses secured by the security interest
Remediate defaults causing collateral disposal or retention
Cannot redeem collateral if written agreement post-default states not to redeem
Grantor's redemption right has priority over other parties' redemption rights

sections.source_quote: Part 4.4 Rules applying after enforcement: (a) by paying the amounts required to discharge the obligations, or by performing the obligations, secured by security interests in the collateral; and (b) by paying the amount of any expenses in relation to the enforcement of the security interest, the payment of which is secured by the security interest.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential financial governance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Registration of security interests across borders
sections.summary: This section establishes rules for registering security interests in personal property located outside Australia, including requirements for financing statements and financing change statements. It specifies that registrations may cover property or persons outside Australia, and outlines data requirements for security interests and prescribed property. The section also details procedures for belief about security interests and registration amendments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section matters for DTIAs as it governs cross-border registration of security interests, affecting data transfers involving international collateral or secured parties.

requirements.text: Registration may cover personal property located outside Australia
Registration may cover persons outside Australia
Financing statements must comply with data requirements for security interests
Applicant must believe on reasonable grounds secured party will take security interest in collateral
Must amend registration within 5 business days if secured party no longer has security interest

sections.source_quote: Section 152: A financing statement, or a financing change statement, may be registered whether or not: (a) the personal property to which the statement relates is located in Australia; or (b) any person who owns or has rights in that property is located in Australia.
sections.relevance_reason: Security registration rules for cross-border financial data

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Verification statements for security registrations
sections.summary: The Registrar must provide verification statements to secured parties before and after registration events, including amendments to addresses or omissions of secured parties. Statement holders must notify grantors of verification statements as soon as reasonably practicable. The Registrar may publish verification statements for multiple events if inconvenient, and contraventions may constitute privacy interference under the Privacy Act 1988.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for verification statements in security registrations, impacting cross-border data transfers involving security interests by defining notification obligations and registration event handling.

requirements.text: Registrar must provide verification statements to secured parties before and after registration events
Verification statements must include data approved by Registrar for registration events
Statement holders must notify grantors of verification statements as soon as reasonably practicable
Verification statements must include all data in the verification statement except specified data
Statement holders may waive right to receive notice if collateral is commercial property
Registrar may publish verification statements for multiple events if inconvenient
Contraventions constitute privacy interference under Privacy Act 1988

sections.source_quote: The Registrar must ensure that a verification statement in relation to a registration event is given to the following persons: (a) a person registered as a secured party in the registration immediately before the time of the registration event; (b) a person registered as a secured party in the registration immediately after the time of the registration event.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party data handling and privacy interference

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Registration timing and effectiveness for security interests
sections.summary: This section defines registration timing, effective periods, and defects in security registrations. Registrations become effective from registration time until the earliest of end time, amendment time, or when registration stops being available for search. Defects in registration may temporarily affect effectiveness but can be corrected within 60 months or 5 business days.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical timing mechanisms for security registrations that impact data transfer impact assessments by establishing when registrations become effective and how defects affect cross-border security interests.

requirements.text: Registration becomes effective from registration time until earliest of end time, amendment time, or when registration stops being available for search
Registration is ineffective due to defect if there is seriously misleading data or specific defects
Defects may temporarily affect registration effectiveness for up to 60 months or 5 business days
Secured party must amend registration within 5 business days if collateral becomes unperfected

sections.source_quote: A registration with respect to a security interest that describes particular collateral, in relation to a secured party, is effective with respect to that collateral from the registration time for the description of the collateral until the earliest of the following times: (a) the end time (if any) registered for the collateral; (b) if the registration is amended to omit the collateral description—the amendment time; (c) the time when the description of the collateral in the registration stops being available for search in the register (by reference to that time) in respect of the secured party.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential security registration timing

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Authorized data access for financial and legal purposes
sections.summary: This section defines permissible purposes for accessing the register to search for data. It specifies that searchers must have a legitimate purpose related to the administration of the Act, enforcement of liens, bankruptcy proceedings, or other authorized activities. The search must not be conducted for unauthorized purposes, and data obtained must be used only for the specified purpose unless lawfully obtained from another source.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for lawful data access that must be considered in cross-border data transfers involving financial records, ensuring compliance with privacy and legal requirements.

requirements.text: Searchers must not access the register otherwise than for the purpose specified in the item
Searchers must not use data obtained from the register otherwise than for the purpose specified in the item unless lawfully obtained from another source

sections.source_quote: Search otherwise than for authorised purpose (3) A searcher mentioned in an item in the table in subsection (2) must not, otherwise than for the purpose specified in the item: (a) access the register to search for data; or (b) use data obtained as a result of accessing the register, unless the searcher has also obtained the data lawfully from another source.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data access with strict purpose limits

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Conditions for third-party data access in financial registries
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions that must be met when accessing third-party data through the register. It specifies that third-party data may be included in verification statements and search results, but only if the data is not personal information under the Privacy Act 1988. The conditions for use of third-party data must be adhered to by applicants to ensure lawful processing.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes conditions on third-party data access that must be evaluated in data transfer impact assessments to ensure compliance with privacy and data use restrictions.

requirements.text: Third party data does not include personal information under the Privacy Act 1988
Third party data conditions must be complied with when applying for registration or access to the register

sections.source_quote: Third party data does not include personal information, within the meaning of the Privacy Act 1988, about an individual.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party data access in financial registries with privacy compliance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Amendment notices and registration changes for secured parties
sections.summary: The Registrar may issue amendment notices to secured parties when amendments are demanded, following a statement from the amendment demand giver. Amendment notices must be issued within 5 business days after receiving a statement, unless initiated by the Registrar. The Registrar must register financing change statements amending registrations if the amendment is authorized under section 178, with exceptions for timely responses from secured parties. The Registrar must consider responses and relevant information when deciding on registration changes. Data removed due to amendments cannot be searched in the register before or after removal if the Registrar decides so.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the process for amending registrations when secured parties require changes, including amendment notices, registration of changes, and data removal. It affects cross-border data transfers by specifying when data can be removed from the register and how it is treated post-removal.

requirements.text: The Registrar may give the secured party an amendment notice stating the amendment demanded
An amendment notice must be issued within 5 business days after receiving a statement unless initiated by the Registrar
The Registrar must register a financing change statement amending the registration if the amendment is authorized under section 178
The Registrar may register a financing change statement before the 5-day period if the secured party has responded and the Registrar has no reason to believe further responses
Data removed from the register due to amendment must not be searchable before or after removal if the Registrar decides so

sections.source_quote: Section 180(1): The Registrar may give the secured party a notice (an amendment notice) of the amendment demanded.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for data compliance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Fees, administrative review and annual reports for registration
sections.summary: This section outlines fee determination for registration and search services, approval of fee payment arrangements, and the obligation to prepare annual reports on the Act's operation. The Registrar must prepare annual reports detailing access refusals and fee structures that do not constitute taxation. The Minister may determine fees through legislative instruments, and the Registrar may approve payment arrangements for these fees.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies fee structures and reporting requirements for data registration, which impacts financial institutions by defining costs for data transfers and maintaining records.

requirements.text: The Minister may determine fees for registration and searching the register through legislative instruments
The Registrar may approve fee payment arrangements for registered persons
The Registrar must prepare annual reports on the Act's operation within a financial year
Annual reports must detail access refusals and confirm fees do not constitute taxation

sections.source_quote: Section 192(1): The Registrar must prepare and give to the Minister an annual report on the operation of this Act as soon as practicable after the end of each financial year.
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: fee structures, annual reports, registration

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Deputy Registrar resignation and termination procedures
sections.summary: This section outlines the resignation process for Deputy Registrars, requiring written resignation to the Minister with effect from receipt or a specified later date. It also details termination conditions, including termination by the Minister via written instrument or if the Deputy Registrar ceases employment under the Public Service Act 1999. These provisions define the administrative framework for Deputy Registrar appointments and their exit.
sections.dtia_summary: These provisions establish the administrative framework for Deputy Registrar appointments and their exit, which may impact the continuity of judicial proceedings and data handling processes in cross-jurisdictional contexts.

requirements.text: A Deputy Registrar may resign by writing signed by him or her and given to the Minister
The resignation takes effect on the day it is received by the Minister or, if a later day is specified in the resignation, on that later day
The Minister may terminate the appointment of a Deputy Registrar by written instrument
The appointment of a Deputy Registrar is terminated if the Deputy Registrar stops being engaged under the Public Service Act 1999 for any reason

sections.source_quote: Section 202(1) and (2): A Deputy Registrar may resign by writing signed by him or her and given to the Minister. The resignation takes effect on the day it is received by the Minister or, if a later day is specified in the resignation, on that later day.
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant administrative procedure

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Court jurisdiction and transfer procedures for PPS matters
sections.summary: This section details the jurisdiction of courts for matters related to security interests in personal property, including the Federal Court, Federal Circuit and Family Courts, and State/Territory courts. It specifies transfer procedures between courts, criteria for transfers, and the role of the Registrar in judicial proceedings. The provisions ensure that PPS matters can be handled efficiently across jurisdictions while maintaining legal continuity.
sections.dtia_summary: These provisions define the jurisdictional framework for court transfers in PPS matters, which may impact data handling and processing across jurisdictions in financial and regulatory contexts.

requirements.text: Jurisdiction is conferred on the Federal Court, the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1), the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), and courts of States and Territories for PPS matters
The Registrar may intervene in judicial proceedings
A court may make a transfer under section 211 on the application of a party or at the court's own initiative
The transferring court must provide all documents filed in the transferring court to the receiving court
The receiving court must proceed as if the proceeding had been originally instituted in the other court

sections.source_quote: Section 205: Jurisdiction is conferred on the Federal Court, the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1), the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), and courts of States and Territories for PPS matters. The Registrar may intervene in judicial proceedings.
sections.relevance_reason: Court procedures for cross-border data transfers

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interest governing law determination
sections.summary: This section establishes rules for determining which jurisdiction's law governs security interests in various property types. It specifies that for goods, the law of the jurisdiction where the goods are located at attachment time governs validity and perfection. For intangible property, the law of the grantor's location governs. Financial property follows similar rules with specific exceptions for ADI accounts and letters of credit. The section also outlines conditions under which parties may agree to use Commonwealth law instead.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines jurisdictional rules for security interests across different property types, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial assets by determining applicable law for security interests in international transactions.

requirements.text: The validity of a security interest in goods is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the goods are located when the security interest attaches
The perfection of a security interest in goods is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the goods are located at the time of perfection
The validity of a security interest in intangible property is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the grantor is located when the security interest attaches
The perfection of a security interest in intangible property is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the grantor is located at the time of perfection
The validity of a security interest in financial property is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the grantor is located when the security interest attaches
The perfection of a security interest in financial property is governed by the law of the jurisdiction where the grantor is located at the time of perfection
A security interest in an ADI account is governed by the law of the jurisdiction that governs the ADI account
Parties may agree in writing that another jurisdiction's law governs an ADI account security interest if the ADI consents and applying that law would not be manifestly contrary to public policy

sections.source_quote: Section 239: The validity of a security interest in intangible property is governed by the law of the jurisdiction (other than the law relating to conflict of laws) in which the grantor is located when the security interest attaches, under that law, to the property.
sections.relevance_reason: Security interest law determination for financial data

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: State referral status and amendment references for personal property
sections.summary: This section defines when a State remains a referring State for personal property matters under the Personal Property Securities Act. A State continues as a referring State if its referral law remains active, its amendment references have not terminated or been restricted, and the relevant version of the Act has not been adopted. It specifies that States stop being referring States when their referral references terminate or become qualified, except for fixtures and transferable water rights which maintain referring status even if amendment references terminate or are restricted.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which States retain or lose their status as referring States for personal property security interests, impacting cross-border data transfers involving security interests in movable assets.

requirements.text: A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that the reference to the Commonwealth of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) terminates in particular circumstances
A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that the adoption of the relevant version of this Act terminates in particular circumstances
A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that any or all of the State's amendment references have not commenced in relation to a particular kind of personal property

A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that the reference to the Commonwealth Parliament of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) has effect only if the matter is not otherwise included in the legislative powers of the Commonwealth Parliament

A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that the reference to the Commonwealth Parliament of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) has effect only if the matter is included in the legislative powers of the Parliament of the State
A State stops being a referring State if the reference to the Commonwealth Parliament terminates
A State stops being a referring State if the adoption of the relevant version of this Act terminates
A State stops being a referring State if the State's amendment reference in relation to personal property (other than fixtures or water rights) terminates
A State stops being a referring State if the State's amendment reference in relation to personal property (other than fixtures or water rights) is qualified or restricted to any degree
A State does not stop being a referring State because the State's amendment reference in relation to fixtures terminates
A State does not stop being a referring State because the State's amendment reference in relation to fixtures is qualified or restricted to any degree
A State does not stop being a referring State because the State's amendment reference in relation to transferable water rights terminates
A State does not stop being a referring State because the State's amendment reference in relation to transferable water rights is qualified or restricted to any degree

sections.source_quote: Section 244(2) of the Personal Property Securities Act 2009: A State is a referring State even if the State's referral law provides that: (a) the reference to the Commonwealth of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) is to terminate in particular circumstances; or (b) the adoption of the relevant version of this Act is to terminate in particular circumstances; or (c) any or all of the State's amendment references have not commenced in relation to a particular kind (or kinds) of personal property (or so commence at or after the registration commencement time); or (d) the reference to the Commonwealth Parliament of a matter covered by subsection (3) or (4) has effect only: (i) if, and to the extent that, the matter is not otherwise included in the legislative powers of the Commonwealth Parliament (otherwise than by a reference under paragraph 51(xxxvii) of the Constitution); or (ii) if and to the extent that the matter is included in the legislative powers of the Parliament of the State.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security agreements: Effect of other laws on security interests
sections.summary: This section establishes that security agreements are subject to other laws where there is inconsistency between the Act and those laws. It specifies that certain Commonwealth Acts, including the Payment Systems and Netting Act 1998, prevail over this Act to the extent of inconsistency. The section also outlines that security agreements are subject to state and territory laws, general law, and regulations, with exceptions for when Division 4 (this Act prevailing) applies. It clarifies that certain effects of laws, such as prohibitions on creating security interests, are excluded from the scope of this section.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by clarifying that security interests may be subject to state and territory laws, requiring assessments to consider how these laws interact with cross-border security agreements and registrations.

requirements.text: If there is inconsistency between this Act and one of the specified Acts, the other Act prevails to the extent of the inconsistency
Subsection 18(1) is subject to each of the following laws: Commonwealth law (other than this Act), State or Territory law, and general law
A law mentioned in subsection (2) does not apply to the extent affected by Division 4 (when this Act prevails) or as prescribed by regulations

sections.source_quote: If there is any inconsistency between this Act and one of the following Acts (the other Act), the other Act prevails to the extent of the inconsistency: (a) the Payment Systems and Netting Act 1998; (b) the Cheques Act 1986; (c) the Bills of Exchange Act 1909; (d) the International Interests in Mobile Equipment (Cape Town Convention) Act 2013.
sections.relevance_reason: Security laws override data compliance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Vesting of unperfected security interests in grantors during bankruptcy
sections.summary: This section details when unperfected security interests vest in the grantor upon the grantor's bankruptcy or winding up. It specifies events such as company winding up, sequestration orders, and bankruptcy under the Bankruptcy Act 1966. The security interest vests immediately before the event occurs, but does not affect title if the person acquires property for new value without knowledge of the event.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it governs the vesting of security interests during bankruptcy, which may impact data transfers involving financial institutions and cross-border assets.

requirements.text: In the event of the bankruptcy of an individual grantor, or the winding up or entry into administration of a body corporate grantor, a secured party's unperfected security interest vests in the grantor
The security interest vests immediately before the event mentioned in paragraph (1)(a) occurs
Subsection (2) does not affect the title of a person to personal property if the person acquires it for new value without knowledge of the event

sections.source_quote: In the event of the bankruptcy of an individual grantor, or the winding up or the entry into administration of a body corporate grantor, a secured party's unperfected security interest vests in the grantor.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: bankruptcy procedures, not data compliance

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Security interests unaffected by section 267
sections.summary: This section specifies security interests that remain unaffected by the vesting rules in section 267. It covers security interests that do not secure payment or performance of obligations, including transfers of accounts or chattel paper, commercial consignments, and security interests governed by foreign jurisdiction law. It also addresses subordinated debts where a junior creditor's obligations are postponed to a senior creditor's debt.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines specific security interests that bypass the vesting rules in section 267, which is critical for DTIAs involving cross-border security interests where foreign jurisdiction law applies or where security interests are subordinated.

requirements.text: Security interests provided for by transactions that do not secure payment or performance of obligations are unaffected by section 267
Security interests governed by foreign jurisdiction law at the time mentioned in paragraph 267(1)(b) are unaffected by section 267
Security interests covered by subsection (2) of this section are unaffected by section 267
A security interest in an account is subordinated if the obligor owes money to a senior creditor and junior creditor
The junior creditor must postpone the obligor's debt to the senior creditor
The junior creditor must transfer property or proceeds to the senior creditor to the value of the debt
The junior creditor must hold property or proceeds on trust for the senior creditor

sections.source_quote: Security interests to which vesting rule does not apply (1) Subsection 267(2) and section 267A (security interests vested in grantor) do not apply to the following security interests: (a) a security interest provided for by any of the following transactions, if the interest does not secure the payment or performance of an obligation: (i) a transfer of an account or chattel paper; (iii) a commercial consignment; (aa) a security interest for which perfection, and the effect of perfection or non-perfection, is governed by the law of a foreign jurisdiction at the time mentioned in paragraph 267(1)(b); (b) a security interest covered by subsection (2) of this section.
sections.relevance_reason: Security interests and cross-border aspects

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Secured parties must disclose information to interested persons
sections.summary: This section outlines the obligations for secured parties to provide information to interested persons upon request. It specifies the types of information that must be disclosed, including security agreements, amounts secured, and property details. It also sets timeframes for responses and conditions under which disclosure may be exempted, such as confidentiality agreements or legal obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires secured parties to disclose specific information to interested persons, which is critical for DTIAs involving cross-border data transfers where security interests may affect data access and control.

requirements.text: Secured parties must provide a copy of the security agreement
Secured parties must provide a statement of the amount or obligation secured by the interest
Secured parties must provide an itemised list of personal property attached to the security interest
Secured parties must respond to requests within 10 business days
Secured parties may indicate a security interest in all present and after-acquired property
Secured parties may exempt responses if a confidentiality agreement exists
Secured parties may exempt responses if the response would contravene law or duty of confidence

sections.source_quote: Secured party to provide certain information relating to security interest (1) An interested person mentioned in subsection (9) may request a secured party who holds a security interest in collateral to send or make available to the interested person, or any other person, any of the following: (a) a copy of the security agreement that provides for the security interest; (b) a statement in writing setting out the amount or the obligation that is secured by the security interest and the terms of payment or performance of the obligation, as at the day specified in the request; (c) a written approval or correction of an itemised list of personal property attached to the request indicating in which items of property the security interest is granted, as at the day specified in the request; (d) a written approval or correction of the following attached to the request, as at the day specified in the request: (i) the amount or the obligation that is secured by the security interest; (ii) the terms of payment or performance of the obligation.
sections.relevance_reason: Security disclosure obligations with timeframes

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Notices to secured parties: delivery methods and recipient requirements
sections.summary: This section specifies how notices must be delivered to registered secured parties: by physical delivery, pre-paid post, fax, or email. It requires notices to be given to each secured party when multiple parties are registered. A single notice can be given to all secured parties if it complies with section 287. The Registrar may approve specific identifiers for notices to ensure they are properly directed to the correct party.
sections.dtia_summary: Notices to secured parties must be delivered via specific methods to ensure proper identification and receipt, which impacts cross-border data transfers involving secured parties.

requirements.text: Notices must be delivered to a secured party by leaving at the address
Notices must be delivered to a secured party by pre-paid post to the address
Notices must be delivered to a secured party by fax or email to the address
A single notice may be given to all secured parties if it complies with section 287

sections.source_quote:
Part 8.5  Notices and timing
Section 288
(a) a registration includes 2 or more secured parties; and
(b) a notice or document is required or permitted to be given to each of the secured parties.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for notices

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Onus of proof and constructive knowledge for secured parties
sections.summary: This section outlines the onus of proof for certain facts in secured transactions, including the attachment of security interests, perfection by registration, and knowledge requirements. It defines constructive knowledge as knowledge that would be had by an honest and prudent person under the circumstances. The onus of proof lies with the person asserting facts related to security interests and knowledge.
sections.dtia_summary: The onus of proof and constructive knowledge rules impact data transfer assessments by defining how knowledge of data is established in cross-border transactions involving secured parties.

requirements.text: The onus of proving security interest attachment lies with the asserting party
The onus of proving security interest perfection by registration lies with the asserting party
The onus of proving free of security interest lies with the asserting party
Constructive knowledge is defined as knowledge an honest and prudent person would have

sections.source_quote:
Part 8.6—Onus of proof and knowledge
Section 295
This Part provides that the onus of proving certain facts lies with the person asserting those facts.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure legal definition of secured transactions, no data protection link

sections.heading: Section 1 (cont.)
sections.title: Initial application of security interests to personal property
sections.summary: This section specifies when the Australian Security Interests Act starts applying to new security agreements, interests arising after commencement, transitional agreements, and personal property data. It outlines specific provisions for enforceability, statutory interests, intellectual property licenses, and the starting time for registrations. The Act applies to security interests created at or after the registration commencement time, with transitional security interests having special rules for enforceability and perfection.
